title: Loops & Logic
subtitle: Think Logically, Loop Smartly, Solve Anything!
overview: Loops and logic are the heartbeat of every program. In this session, students deepen their understanding of how computers make decisions using Boolean logic and truth tables, and how loops are used to solve real problems efficiently. Through hands-on challenges — from FizzBuzz to pattern printing to mini games — students build the logical thinking skills that underpin all of programming.

keyTopics: Boolean Logic — All decisions in computing reduce to True or False; named after mathematician George Boole
AND Gate — Output is True ONLY when BOTH inputs are True; like needing both a key AND a PIN to open a safe
OR Gate — Output is True when AT LEAST ONE input is True; like a door that opens with card OR fingerprint
NOT Gate — Flips the input: NOT True = False, NOT False = True; like an inverter switch
Truth Table — A table listing every possible input combination and the resulting output for a logic operation
Compound Conditions — Combining multiple conditions using and, or, not: `if age >= 13 and age < 18`
Short-Circuit Evaluation — Python stops evaluating `and` as soon as one part is False, and `or` as soon as one part is True
for Loop with range() — Repeating code a fixed number of times; range(start, stop, step) controls the sequence
while Loop with Flag — Using a boolean variable (flag) as the loop condition: `running = True` … `running = False` to exit
Loop Counter Pattern — Accumulating a result inside a loop: sum, count, product, maximum, minimum
FizzBuzz — Classic problem: print numbers 1–100, replacing multiples of 3 with Fizz, 5 with Buzz, and 15 with FizzBuzz
Modulo Operator (%) — Returns the remainder of division: `10 % 3 = 1`, `15 % 5 = 0`; essential for even/odd and FizzBuzz checks
Nested Loop Logic — Outer loop controls rows, inner loop controls columns — used for patterns, grids, and tables
Loop Invariant — A condition that stays true before and after every loop iteration; key concept in logical reasoning about loops

activity.title: Logic Gate Cards + FizzBuzz + Pattern Challenge
parts: Index cards labelled TRUE and FALSE (for logic gate activity)
Printed FizzBuzz and pattern challenge sheets
steps: Part 1 — Human Logic Gates: In pairs, students hold TRUE or FALSE cards. Teacher calls an operation (AND / OR / NOT). Students work out the output and hold up the result card. Work through all combinations in the truth table
Part 2 — Compound Conditions: Write Python expressions using and, or, not and predict the output before running: `True and False`, `not True or False`, `(5 > 3) and (10 < 20)`
Part 3 — Modulo Practice: Explore the % operator — print results of 10%3, 15%5, 7%2, 100%10. Discuss: when does % return 0? How can we use this to check for multiples?

Part 4 — FizzBuzz Challenge: Write a for loop from 1 to 100. If the number is divisible by both 3 and 5, print FizzBuzz. If by 3 only, print Fizz. If by 5 only, print Buzz. Otherwise print the number. Hint: check the combined condition FIRST
Part 5 — Accumulator Loop: Use a for loop to calculate the sum of all even numbers from 1 to 50. Then find the largest number in a list without using max()
Part 6 — Pattern Printing: Use nested for loops to print a right-angled triangle of stars (row 1 has 1 star, row 2 has 2 stars … row 5 has 5 stars). Then try an inverted triangle

Part 7 — Number Guessing Game: Use random.randint() to pick a secret number 1–20. Use a while loop to keep asking the player to guess. Print 'Too high!', 'Too low!', or 'Correct!' and count how many guesses it took
Challenge: Extend the guessing game — limit the player to 5 attempts using a counter variable. If they run out, reveal the number and print 'Game Over!'

quiz.q: What is Boolean Logic named after?
options: A programming language called Bool
The mathematician George Boole who developed the algebra of logic in the 1800s
A type of computer circuit invented in Silicon Valley
The Boolean Sea where early computers were built
quiz.explanation: Boolean Logic is named after George Boole, a 19th-century mathematician who created an algebra using only True and False. His work became the foundation of all digital circuits and computer logic!

quiz.q: What is the output of `True AND False`?
options: True
False
True and False together
Error
quiz.explanation: AND requires BOTH inputs to be True. True AND False → False. Think of it as needing both a key AND a PIN — if either is missing, the safe stays locked. In Python: `True and False` → `False`.

quiz.q: What is the output of `False OR True`?
options: False
True
Neither
Error
quiz.explanation: OR only needs ONE input to be True. False OR True → True. Think of it as a door that opens with a card OR a fingerprint — either one is enough. In Python: `False or True` → `True`.

quiz.q: What does the NOT operator do?
options: Adds a condition to an if statement
Flips the Boolean value: NOT True becomes False, and NOT False becomes True
Checks if two values are not equal
Stops a loop from running
quiz.explanation: NOT is an inverter! `not True` → `False`, `not False` → `True`. It flips the value like a light switch — whatever it was, it becomes the opposite. In Python: `not (5 > 3)` → `not True` → `False`.

quiz.q: What does the Modulo operator (%) do?
options: Multiplies two numbers together
Divides two numbers and returns the quotient
Divides two numbers and returns the REMAINDER
Rounds a number to the nearest integer
quiz.explanation: % returns the remainder after division! `10 % 3 = 1` (10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1). `15 % 5 = 0` (divides perfectly, no remainder). When `n % x == 0`, n is exactly divisible by x — the key to FizzBuzz!

quiz.q: In FizzBuzz, why must you check `n % 15 == 0` (or `n % 3 == 0 and n % 5 == 0`) FIRST?
options: Because 15 is the largest number
Because Python always checks the last condition
If you check n % 3 first, multiples of 15 will print Fizz and never reach the FizzBuzz condition
The order of conditions does not matter in Python
quiz.explanation: Order matters with elif! If `n % 3 == 0` is checked first, the number 15 satisfies it and prints Fizz — it never reaches the FizzBuzz check. Always test the most specific condition (divisible by BOTH 3 and 5) first!

`range(start, stop, step)` → start at 1, stop before 10, step by 2 = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9. The third argument is the step size! `range(0, 11, 2)` gives even numbers 0–10. `range(10, 0, -1)` counts backwards!

quiz.q: What is an Accumulator Pattern in a loop?
options: A loop that runs faster and faster each iteration
Using a variable that starts at 0 and collects (accumulates) a running total inside the loop
A pattern that prints accumulating rows of stars
A loop that accumulates error messages
quiz.explanation: The accumulator pattern is one of the most useful in programming! Start with `total = 0`, then inside the loop: `total += number`. After the loop, total holds the sum of everything. Use it for sums, products, counts, and averages.

options: A flag printed inside the loop output
A boolean variable used as the while loop condition, set to False when you want the loop to stop
An error that stops the loop unexpectedly
A special keyword that marks the end of a loop
quiz.explanation: A flag variable controls a while loop cleanly! `running = True` … `while running:` … `if condition: running = False`. When the flag flips to False, the loop exits naturally. Great for menus and games where you don't know the iteration count in advance.

quiz.q: What is Short-Circuit Evaluation in Python logic?
options: When a loop runs too fast and skips iterations
Python stops evaluating an `and` expression as soon as one part is False, or an `or` expression as soon as one part is True
When the program short-circuits and crashes
A method to reduce the number of loop iterations
quiz.explanation: Python is efficient! For `A and B`: if A is False, B is never checked — the result is already False. For `A or B`: if A is True, B is never checked — the result is already True. This saves time and prevents errors like checking index before accessing a list.

quiz.q: In a nested loop that prints a 5-row star triangle, how many total stars are printed?
quiz.explanation: Row 1: 1 star, Row 2: 2 stars, Row 3: 3 stars, Row 4: 4 stars, Row 5: 5 stars → total = 1+2+3+4+5 = 15 stars. This is also the formula n×(n+1)/2, where n=5 → 5×6/2 = 15!

quiz.q:
What is the output of this code?
`for i in range(1, 6):`
`    if i % 2 == 0:`
`        print(i)`
quiz.explanation: range(1,6) gives 1,2,3,4,5. `i % 2 == 0` checks for even numbers — those with no remainder when divided by 2. Only 2 and 4 are even in that range, so only 2 and 4 are printed.

quiz.q: A while loop runs forever and freezes the program. What is the most likely cause?
options: The loop body has too many print statements
The loop condition never becomes False because the variable controlling it is never updated inside the loop
Python cannot handle while loops longer than 100 iterations
The indentation of the loop body is wrong
quiz.explanation: An infinite loop happens when the exit condition can never be reached! If `while count < 10:` but you forgot to write `count += 1` inside the loop, count stays at 0 forever. Always make sure something inside the loop moves you toward the exit condition!

quiz.q: In a number guessing game using a while loop, what is the best way to limit the player to 5 attempts?
options: Use a for loop with range(5) instead of a while loop
Add a counter variable that increments each guess, and add `and counter < 5` to the while condition
Use break after every guess
Restart the program after 5 seconds using a timer
quiz.explanation: Both approaches work! A counter with `while guess != secret and attempts < 5:` lets the loop exit for either reason — correct guess or 5 attempts used. After the loop, check which condition ended it to print the right message.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between using `and` and `or` when combining conditions?
options: and is used for numbers, or is used for strings
and requires ALL conditions to be True; or requires only ONE condition to be True
and is faster than or in Python
There is no practical difference between and and or
quiz.explanation: `and` = strictest gate — ALL must be True. `or` = most lenient gate — ANY one True is enough. Example: a theme park ride requires `height >= 120 and age >= 10` (both). A free-entry day is `is_student or is_senior` (either one qualifies).
